en.extract:
The Indian golden oriole (Oriolus kundoo) is a species of oriole found in the Indian subcontinent and Central Asia. The species was formerly considered to be a subspecies of the Eurasian golden oriole, but is now considered a full species. Adults can be told apart from the Eurasian golden oriole by the black of the eye stripe extending behind the eye.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Indian golden oriole was described by the English naturalist William Henry Sykes in 1832 and given the binomial name  Oriolus kundoo. Although initially described as a separate species, the Indian golden oriole was usually treated as a subspecies of the Eurasian golden oriole. In 2005, the ornithologists Pamela Rasmussen and John Anderton in their Birds of South Asia decided to treat the two varieties as separate species based on the differences in morphology, plumage, calls and the fact that the two varieties do not intergrade. Support for this split was provided by a molecular phylogenetic study published in 2010, and most ornithologists now treat the Indian golden oriole as a separate species. The race baltistanicus was judged by Charles Vaurie to be indistinguishable from the nominate subspecies and the Indian golden oriole is considered to be monotypic.


Description

The Indian golden oriole is very similar to the Eurasian golden oriole but has more yellow in the tail and has a paler shade of red in the iris and bill. The male has the black eye stripe extending behind the eye, a large carpal patch on the wing and wide yellow tips to the secondaries and tertiaries. The streaks on the underside of females is more sharp than in the females of the Eurasian golden oriole. The European species is larger with a wing length of 149–162 mm in adult males compared to 136–144 mm in O. kundoo. The wing formula is also different with primary 2 longer than 5 in O. oriolus while primary 5 is longer than 2 in O. kundoo.


Distribution and habitat

This oriole breeds from Baluchistan and Afghanistan along the Himalayas to Nepal. Some populations breed in the peninsular region but are very local. The northern populations winter in southern India, with some birds wintering in Sri Lanka. The populations that occur in the Maldives and the Andaman Islands have not been carefully examined.
The Indian golden oriole inhabits a range of habitats including open deciduous forests, semi-evergreen forests, woodland, forest edge, mangroves, open country with scattered trees, parks, gardens, orchards, and plantations.


Behaviour and ecology
The Indian golden oriole's flight is dipping but strong and has been recorded to reach about 40 km/h (25 mph). It sometimes bathes by repeatedly flying into a small pool of water. An individual ringed in Gujarat was recovered in Tajikistan more than nine years later.


Breeding
The Indian golden oriole is a partial migrant. It breeds in central Asia and the Indian subcontinent. The Indian populations are largely resident while the other populations are migratory. The breeding season is April to August, the nest being a small cup placed in a fork near the end of a branch. Nests are often built in the vicinity of the nest of a black drongo. Two or three white eggs with reddish, brown, and black speckling form the typical clutch. Both parents take part in nest and brood care, defending the nest against intruding birds such as shikras and crows.


Food and feeding
Orioles feed on fruits, nectar, and insects. They are capable of dispersing the seeds of many berry-bearing plants including the invasive Lantana camara. An oriole has been recorded preying on southern flying lizards.


Threats
A protozoal blood parasite, Haemoproteus orioli, described from this species has been suggested to occur in many oriole species but may represent different lineages.


References

The '''Indian golden oriole''' ('''''Oriolus kundoo''''') is a species of [[Old World oriole|oriole]] found in the [[Indian subcontinent]] and [[Central Asia]]. The species was formerly considered to be a subspecies of the [[Eurasian golden oriole]], but is now considered a full species. Adults can be told apart from the Eurasian golden oriole by the black of the eye stripe extending behind the eye.

==Taxonomy and systematics==
The Indian golden oriole was [[Species description|described]] by the English naturalist [[William Henry Sykes]] in 1832 and given the [[binomial name]] '' Oriolus kundoo''.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Sykes | first=William Henry | author-link= William Henry Sykes | year=1832 | title=60. ''Oriolus kundoo'' | journal= Proceedings of the Committee of Science and Correspondence of the Zoological Society of London | issue=2 | page=87 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/26730762 }}</ref> Although initially described as a separate species, the Indian golden oriole was usually treated as a [[subspecies]] of the [[Eurasian golden oriole]].<ref>{{cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr  | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1962 | title=Check-list of birds of the world | volume=15 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=127 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14485500 }}</ref> In 2005, the ornithologists [[Pamela C. Rasmussen|Pamela Rasmussen]] and John Anderton in their ''[[Birds of South Asia. The Ripley Guide|Birds of South Asia]]'' decided to treat the two varieties as separate species based on the differences in morphology, plumage, calls and the fact that the two varieties do not intergrade.<ref name=pcr>{{cite book | last1=Rasmussen | first1=Pamela C. | author1-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | last2=Anderton | first2=John C. | year=2005 | title=Birds of South Asia. The Ripley Guide | volume=2 | publisher=Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History and Lynx Edicions | place=Washington D.C. and Barcelona | isbn=978-84-87334-66-5 | page=586}}</ref> Support for this split was provided by a [[molecular phylogenetic]] study published in 2010,<ref name=jonsson>{{cite journal |last1=Jønsson | first1=K.A. | last2=Bowie | first2=R.C.K. | last3=Moyle | first3=R.G. | last4=Irestedt | first4=M. | last5=Christidis | first5=L. | last6=Norman | first6=J.A. | last7=Fjeldså | first7=J. | year=2010 | title=Phylogeny and biogeography of Oriolidae (Aves: Passeriformes) | journal=Ecography | volume=33 | issue=2 | pages=232–241 |doi=10.1111/j.1600-0587.2010.06167.x | doi-access=free | bibcode=2010Ecogr..33..232J }}</ref> and most ornithologists now treat the Indian golden oriole as a separate species.<ref>{{cite web | last=Lepage | first=Denis | title=Indian Golden-Oriole ''Oriolus kundoo'' Sykes, 1832 | publisher=Avibase | url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?lang=EN&avibaseid=4C263A0E9863761B | access-date=20 December 2017 }}</ref> The race ''baltistanicus'' was judged by [[Charles Vaurie]] to be indistinguishable from the [[nominate subspecies]] and the Indian golden oriole is considered to be [[monotypic]].<ref>{{cite journal| last=Vaurie | first=Charles | year=1958 |  title=Systematic notes on Palearctic birds. No. 32, Oriolidae, Dicruridae, Bombycillidae, Pycnonotidae, Nectariniidae, and Zosteropidae | journal=American Museum Novitates | issue=1869 | pages=1–28 | hdl=2246/3645 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2017 | title=Orioles, drongos & fantails | work=World Bird List Version 7.3 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/orioles/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=20 November 2017 }}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Eurasian Golden Oriole (Oriolus oriolus) on nest W IMG 9050.jpg|thumb|left|upright|Male in nest (Hyderabad, India)]]
The Indian golden oriole is very similar to the Eurasian golden oriole but has more yellow in the tail and has a paler shade of red in the iris and bill. The male has the black eye stripe extending behind the eye, a large carpal patch on the wing and wide yellow tips to the secondaries and tertiaries. The streaks on the underside of females is more sharp than in the females of the Eurasian golden oriole.<ref name=pcr/><ref name=jonsson/><ref>{{cite journal| journal =Journal of Ornithology| volume= 64| issue=2| pages= 241–243 |doi= 10.1007/BF02250522| title= Einige Bemerkungen über ''Oriolus oriolus kundoo'' Sykes| language= de| author=Kollibay, P. |year= 1915 |s2cid= 35008333 |url= https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/partpdf/142437}}</ref> The European species is larger with a wing length of 149–162 mm in adult males compared to 136–144 mm in ''O. kundoo''. The wing formula is also different with primary 2 longer than 5 in ''O. oriolus'' while primary 5 is longer than 2 in ''O. kundoo''.

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Indian Golden Oriole in Kuldiha Wildlife Sanctuary March 2025 by Tisha Mukherjee 01.jpg|thumb|Indian Golden Oriole (Oriolus kundoo) female on [[erythrina variegata]] in [[Kuldiha Wildlife Sanctuary]], Odisha, India.]]
This oriole breeds from [[Baluchistan]] and Afghanistan along the [[Himalaya]]s to Nepal. Some populations breed in the peninsular region but are very local. The northern populations winter in southern India, with some birds wintering in Sri Lanka. The populations that occur in the [[Maldives]] and the [[Andaman Islands]] have not been carefully examined.<ref name=pcr/>

The Indian golden oriole inhabits a range of habitats including open deciduous forests, semi-evergreen forests, woodland, forest edge, mangroves, open country with scattered trees, parks, gardens, orchards, and plantations.<ref name = "HBW">{{Cite book | first1 =B. | last1=Walther | first2=P.| last2=Jones | editor-last=J. | editor-first=del Hoyo | editor2-last=A. | editor2-first=Elliott | editor3-last=D. | editor3-first=Christie | chapter=Family Oriolidae (Orioles and Figbirds) | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World |volume=13: Penduline-tits to Shrikes | year=2008 | pages=692–723 | place=Barcelona | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-96553-45-3}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
The Indian golden oriole's flight is dipping but strong and has been recorded to reach about {{cvt|40|km/h}}. It sometimes bathes by repeatedly flying into a small pool of water. An individual ringed in Gujarat was recovered in [[Tajikistan]] more than nine years later.<ref name=hbk>{{cite book|last1=Ali | first1=S. | last2=Ripley | first2=S.D. | year=1987 |title=Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan |volume=5 |edition=Second | publisher=Oxford University Press| place=New Delhi |chapter= |pages=102–104}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Ambedkar, V.C. |year=1986|title=Recovery of an Indian Golden Oriole (''Oriolus oriolus kundoo'') in the U.S.S.R. |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=83 |issue=Supplement |pages= 211–212|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48772403}}</ref>

===Breeding===
The Indian golden oriole is a partial [[bird migration|migrant]]. It breeds in central Asia and the Indian subcontinent. The Indian populations are largely resident while the other populations are migratory.<ref name = "HBW"/> The breeding season is April to August, the nest being a small cup placed in a fork near the end of a branch. Nests are often built in the vicinity of the nest of a [[black drongo]].<ref name=pcr/> Two or three white eggs with reddish, brown, and black speckling form the typical clutch. Both parents take part in nest and brood care, defending the nest against intruding birds such as [[shikra]]s and crows.<ref name=hbk/>

===Food and feeding===
Orioles feed on fruits, nectar, and insects.<ref name=pcr/> They are capable of dispersing the seeds of many berry-bearing plants including the invasive ''[[Lantana camara]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal| volume = 4| pages = 472–478| last = Ali| first=S. | title = Economic ornithology in India| journal = Current Science | year = 1936| url=http://www.ias.ac.in/jarch/currsci/4/472-478.pdf}}</ref> An oriole has been recorded preying on [[southern flying lizard]]s.<ref>{{cite journal|author= Balachandran, S. |year=1998 | title=Golden oriole ''Oriolus oriolus'' preying on flying lizard ''Draco dussumieri'' Dum. & Bibr| journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=95 |issue= 1| page=115 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48604635 }}</ref>

===Threats===
A protozoal blood parasite, ''Haemoproteus orioli'', described from this species has been suggested to occur in many oriole species<ref>{{cite journal| last=Peirce | first=M.A. | year= 1984 |volume =18| issue= 5| title=Haematozoa of Zambian birds VII. Redescription of ''Haemoproteus orioli'' from ''Oriolus oriolus'' (Oriolidae)| doi=10.1080/00222938400770651 | pages=785–787| journal=Journal of Natural History| bibcode=1984JNatH..18..785P }}</ref> but may represent different lineages.<ref>{{cite journal| last1=Dimitrov | first1=D. | last2=Zehtindjiev | first2=P. | last3=Bensch | first3=S. | year=2010 | title=Genetic diversity of avian blood parasites in SE Europe: Cytochrome b lineages of the genera ''Plasmodium'' and ''Haemoproteus'' (Haemosporida) from Bulgaria | journal=Acta Parasitologica | volume =55| issue= 3| pages=201–209 |doi=10.2478/s11686-010-0029-z | s2cid=19153477| doi-access=free | bibcode=2010AcPar..55029.zD }}</ref>

zh.extract:
印度金黄鹂（台湾作印度黃鸝）（学名：Oriolus kundoo），是雀形目黄鹂科的一种鸟类。


特征
印度金黄鹂体重约65.6克，翼长约138.9毫米，嘴峰长约30.9毫米，喙宽度约9.2毫米，喙厚度约8.7毫米，跗蹠长约23.6毫米，尾长约84.6毫米。印度金黄鹂是候鸟，其栖息在密集的中等高度的林地中，食性为杂食性。


分布
西西伯利亚至印度次大陆。


参考资料
